cards.front: What is the Italian Game opening?
cards.back: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 — White develops the bishop to c4, targeting f7 (the weakest square in Black's position).
cards.notes: One of the oldest openings. Leads to open, tactical games. Common continuation: 3...Bc5 (Giuoco Piano) or 3...Nf6 (Two Knights Defense).

cards.back: 1.e4 c5 — Black's most popular response to 1.e4. Creates an asymmetric pawn structure, leading to sharp tactical play.
cards.notes: Variations: Najdorf (2...d6, 5...a6), Dragon (5...g6), Scheveningen (5...e6), Classical. Most played defense at grandmaster level.

cards.front: What is a fork in chess?
cards.back: A tactic where one piece attacks two or more enemy pieces simultaneously. The opponent can only save one, losing material.
cards.notes: Knight forks are most common (due to unique movement). Royal fork: knight attacks king and queen simultaneously. All pieces can fork.

cards.front: What is a pin in chess?
cards.back: A tactic where an attacking piece (bishop, rook, or queen) immobilizes an enemy piece because moving it would expose a more valuable piece behind it. An absolute pin (against the king) makes the pinned piece legally unable to move.
cards.notes: Example: Bishop on g5 pins a knight on f6 to the queen on d8. Relative pin: piece CAN move but shouldn't. Absolute pin: piece CANNOT move (exposes king).

cards.front: What is a skewer in chess?
cards.back: A tactic where an attacking piece threatens a valuable piece, forcing it to move, and then captures the less valuable piece behind it. The reverse of a pin.
cards.notes: Example: Rook checks king on e8, king moves, rook captures rook on e1. Only long-range pieces (bishop, rook, queen) can skewer.

cards.back: 1.d4 d5 2.c4 — White offers a pawn sacrifice to gain central control. Not a true gambit because White can usually regain the pawn.
cards.notes: QGD (Declined): 2...e6 (solid, most popular). QGA (Accepted): 2...dxc4. Slav Defense: 2...c6. Very popular after the Netflix series.

cards.front: What is the Ruy Lopez opening?
cards.back: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bb5 — White puts pressure on the knight defending e5. One of the oldest and most deeply analyzed openings.
cards.notes: Also called the Spanish Game. 3...a6 (Morphy Defense) is most common. Berlin Defense (3...Nf6): very solid, popularized by Kramnik vs Kasparov 2000.

cards.front: What is castling and when can you castle?
cards.back: Special king move: King moves 2 squares toward a rook, rook jumps to the other side. Kingside: O-O. Queenside: O-O-O. Requirements: King and rook haven't moved, no pieces between them, king not in check, king doesn't pass through or land on attacked square.
cards.notes: Castle early for king safety. Queenside castling can be used as an attacking setup. You CAN castle if the rook passes through an attacked square.

cards.back: A tactic where moving one piece reveals an attack from another piece behind it. A discovered check occurs when the revealed attack is on the king.
cards.notes: Powerful because the moving piece can simultaneously create its own threat. Double check: both the moving piece and revealed piece give check (king MUST move).

cards.front: What is the en passant rule?
cards.back: If a pawn advances two squares from its starting position and lands beside an enemy pawn, the enemy pawn can capture it as if it had only moved one square. Must be done immediately on the next move or the right is lost.
cards.notes: Only available on the very next move. One of the most forgotten rules. Notation: e.g., exd6 e.p. Often seen in the French Defense and Sicilian.

cards.back: A situation where any move a player makes worsens their position, but they are compelled to move (no passing allowed in chess). Common in endgames.
cards.notes: German for 'compulsion to move.' Classic example: King and pawn endgames where the side to move loses. Recognizing zugzwang is key to winning many endgames.

cards.front: What is the King's Indian Defense?
cards.back: 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 g6 — Black allows White to build a big center, then counterattacks it. Black fianchettoes the bishop to g7.
cards.notes: Aggressive, dynamic defense. Famous practitioners: Kasparov, Fischer, Nakamura. Main line: 3.Nc3 Bg7 4.e4 d6 5.Nf3 O-O.

cards.front: What are the basic checkmate patterns?
cards.back: Back rank mate: Queen or rook delivers check on the 8th rank with the king trapped behind its own pawns. Scholar's mate: 1.e4 e5 2.Bc4 Nc6 3.Qh5 Nf6?? 4.Qxf7#. Smothered mate: Knight delivers checkmate while the king is surrounded by its own pieces.
cards.notes: Other key patterns: Anastasia's mate, Arabian mate, Boden's mate, Epaulette mate. Pattern recognition dramatically improves tactical vision.

cards.back: 1.d4, 2.Bf4 (or 2.Nf3 then 3.Bf4) — A solid, systematic opening for White. Bishop goes to f4 before playing e3. Leads to a comfortable, strategic game.
cards.notes: Very popular at club level. Easy to learn — same setup against almost anything. Can be boring but very practical. Popularized by GM Kamsky and IM Bartholomew.

cards.front: What is insufficient material for checkmate?
cards.back: King vs King. King + Bishop vs King. King + Knight vs King. King + Bishop vs King + Bishop (same-colored bishops). These positions are automatic draws.
cards.notes: K+N+N vs K can theoretically checkmate but cannot force it (draw by rule). K+B+N vs K CAN force checkmate (but it's difficult — must be learned).
